claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 5 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 10 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 5 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 10 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To begin, I need to understand your situation. 

**First, let me ask about the child or children you're claiming for:**

1. **What is the name and age of each child** you want to claim Child Benefit for?

This is important because eligibility rules differ based on the child's age:
- Children **under 16** may automatically qualify (with some conditions)
- Young people **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Those **aged 20 or over** generally don't qualify

Please tell me about each child you're inquiring about, including their name and age.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I'm inquiring about my two children - Alex, who is 5 years old, and Blake, who is 10 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'm happy to help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex (age 5) and Blake (age 10).

Since both Alex and Blake are under 16 years old, they fall into the category that may automatically qualify. Let me continue with the assessment.

**Next question: Are either Alex or Blake working full-time?**

By "full-time," I mean working 24 hours or more per week in paid employment. This is very uncommon for children of their age, but I need to confirm this to proceed with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them work at all. They're just at school - Alex is in primary school and Blake is too. They're way too young to be working!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's perfectly clear, thank you! You're absolutely right - at ages 5 and 10, they're focused on their education, which is exactly as it should be.

Since both Alex and Blake are under 16 and not working full-time, they meet the basic qualifying child criteria. Now let me move forward with checking the other eligibility requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are Alex or Blake receiving any benefits in their own right?**

I'm asking specifically about benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit that might be claimed in the child's own name. This is quite rare for young children, but I need to confirm that neither Alex nor Blake is receiving these types of benefits directly.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receive any benefits in their own names. They're just kids - I'm the one who handles all that sort of thing for the family.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that makes sense. As their parent, you would be handling any benefits for the family.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are Alex or Blake enrolled in any type of apprenticeship program?**

Again, this is not typical for children their age (5 and 10), but I need to confirm they're not in any apprenticeship schemes before moving forward.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not. They're 5 and 10 - they're in primary school! Apprenticeships are for teenagers, aren't they? Neither of them are in anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: You're absolutely right! Apprenticeships are indeed for much older young people, typically 16+. I appreciate your patience with these questions - I need to go through each step to ensure a thorough assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now we're moving on to questions about **your situation as the claimant**.

**Residency Question: Do you normally live in the UK?**

To qualify for Child Benefit, you need to meet UK residency requirements. This can be satisfied in several ways:
- You normally live in the UK, OR
- You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, OR
- You have pre-settled status and meet certain conditions (working, studying, etc.), OR
- You're a Crown servant living abroad

Do you normally live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. We're all here together as a family.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since you normally live in the UK, you meet the residency requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Responsibility Question: Are you responsible for Alex and Blake?**

To be considered responsible for a child, you need to meet at least one of these conditions:
- You **live with the child**, OR
- You **contribute to their upkeep** (paying at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their care - things like food, clothes, housing, pocket money), OR
- You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

Since you mentioned you all live together as a family, it sounds like you live with both Alex and Blake. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. Both Alex and Blake live with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since you live with both Alex and Blake, you clearly meet the responsibility requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Important Question: Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time. For example, this could be relevant if:
- You share care with another parent or guardian
- The children's other parent is claiming
- Another family member (like a grandparent) has a claim

Are you aware of anyone else claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them. It's just me - I'm the one looking after them and handling everything.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake, we can continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Child's Location Question: Are Alex or Blake currently:**
- In hospital (as an inpatient)?
- In local authority care?
- In a foster care arrangement?
- Or are they living with you normally at home?

This is important because there are special rules that apply if a child is in hospital or care for extended periods.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: They're both just living with me normally at home. Neither of them are in hospital or care or anything like that - they're healthy and happy at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's wonderful to hear that they're both healthy and happy at home with you!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Final Question: Is the local council paying towards Alex or Blake's accommodation or maintenance?**

This would typically apply in situations where the council is providing financial support for the child's care or living costs. Since you've mentioned they live with you at home and you're looking after them, I expect the answer is no, but I need to confirm this final point.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards them. I'm supporting them myself - they live with me and I cover all their costs.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Excellent News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

Based on the information you've provided, **you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake**.

### Why You're Eligible:

**For Alex (age 5):**
- ✅ Under 16 years old
- ✅ Not working
- ✅ Not receiving benefits in their own right
- ✅ You live with and are responsible for Alex
- ✅ No one else is claiming for Alex
- ✅ Living normally at home with you
- ✅ Council not paying towards their care

**For Blake (age 10):**
- ✅ Under 16 years old
- ✅ Not working
- ✅ Not receiving benefits in their own right
- ✅ You live with and are responsible for Blake
- ✅ No one else is claiming for Blake
- ✅ Living normally at home with you
- ✅ Council not paying towards their care

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and both children
3. **Provide birth certificates** (or adoption certificates if applicable) for Alex and Blake
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge**: If you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay some or all of the benefit back through your tax return. You can still claim and choose not to receive payments if you wish (to protect your National Insurance credits).
5. **Payments**: Child Benefit is typically paid every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### How to Apply:
Visit: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or the application process?

reasonings: Alex (age 5) is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) Alex is under 16 years old, meeting the age requirement; (2) Alex is not working full-time (not in any paid employment); (3) Alex is not receiving benefits in their own right; (4) Alex is not in any apprenticeship program; (5) The claimant lives with Alex and is responsible for them; (6) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (7) Alex lives normally at home (not in hospital or care); (8) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

Blake (age 10) is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) Blake is under 16 years old, meeting the age requirement; (2) Blake is not working full-time (not in any paid employment); (3) Blake is not receiving benefits in their own right; (4) Blake is not in any apprenticeship program; (5) The claimant lives with Blake and is responsible for them; (6) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake; (7) Blake lives normally at home (not in hospital or care); (8) The local council is not paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both children, Alex (age 5) and Blake (age 10). Both children meet all the qualifying child criteria as they are under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in apprenticeships. The claimant meets all the claimant requirements: they normally live in the UK, live with and are responsible for both children, no one else is claiming for these children, both children live normally at home, and the local council is not paying towards their care. All eligibility requirements under the UK Child Benefit rules are satisfied for both children.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

All eligibility criteria are met.

Blake (age 10) is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) Blake is under 16 years old, meeting the age requirement; (2) Blake is not working full-time (not in any paid employment); (3) Blake is not receiving benefits in their own right; (4) Blake is not in any apprenticeship program; (5) The claimant lives with Blake and is responsible for them; (6) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake; (7) Blake lives normally at home (not in hospital or care); (8) The local council is not paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

The claimant meets all the claimant requirements: they normally live in the UK, live with and are responsible for both children, no one else is claiming for these children, both children live normally at home, and the local council is not paying towards their care.
